author: @waynegnarlie1
text: I've been experimenting with 0.5mm x 80mm to 150mm aluminum discs painted with previous release of Rob's conductive ink, SECURELY attaching 6 N45 magnets in a ring formation near the diameter and a dc motor via a shaft adapter.  Then I energize with 20KVAC, 5mA and spin starting at low RPM.  Let's just say the effect is interesting.  Perhaps the ink is offering more electron charge than the unpainted disc?  Before sticking my neck out too far, I'm ordering better measurement gear as soon as I can, but it does seem to force electrons into a plane outward in all directions perpendicular to the axis of spin. It also renders my existing test equipment and nearby computers useless, haha!  Test chamber needed.  Field location was tested with small fluorescent tube.   DISCLAIMER: Preliminary observations pending meaningful measurements.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hiya mate - we are no longer making version one we are only making version two now - but your best bet is to mix manganese dioxide in with the ink that way you have a positive and negative thermal coefficient material in the same ink and that will add a huge safety factor - basically it can't get above set temperatures - you do that by altering the amount of manganese dioxide in the mix for a given temperature range - as you are a member I can do a video on this if you like

author: @Graham_Wideman
text: Well done on improving conductivity. But a question about the measurement technique (ohms per square). This depends on the film thickness; you mentioned 8 to 10 microns, but how do you control that? For example, your new results of 8 ohms/square (compared to 36 ohms) _could_ result from using the exact same ink, but applying it 4+ times as thick.  I'm not suggesting that you did that, just that the result would be even more impressive with a confirmation that the thickness was comparable.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no worries mate - but to be honest it would be really dumb of me to cheat on that as it really is easily verifiable and I would get a ton of complaints and a huge loss of integrity as this ink is used by a lot of folks - any way the easiest way is to lay down two strip of tape and pass a roller over the tapes - it's a cut down version of the Meyer rod technique

author: @benthere8051
text:
I sleep very little also. Fortunately, there's an incredible resource called the Internet and technologists like you to keep me informed. It's almost a global conscience. I enjoy your videos.
Questions:
Is your ink stable without baking or sealing?
Does the resistance change in humid environments?
If it is applied between two clean conductors on a PC board, are the connections stable? 
Is it sensitive to the type of metal used as a termination point?
Can you use copper, tin, lead, aluminum, brass as a terminal?
Does it matter if the substrate is porous like paper or does it have to be nonporous like glass or plastic? (do we have to characterize per application?)
Can it be sealed with paint or with plastic, like polyurethane or epoxy?
Can it be silk-screened onto a substrate?
Does the resistance change if successive applications are made? 
(If one application gives you 8 ohms/square will two applications give to 4 ohms?)
Price?
Thanks in advance. I have several applications in mind.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is just paint and let air dry mate - it will vary with humidity - yes but you could lacquer over to stop that. It can be silk screened and thickness will change resistance as resistance is a geometric property - resistivity isn't that's a property of the material - as for the other question - basically you are going to have to try it as I don't know - there are so many applications I haven't tried them - indeed it is impossible to do so - as for price - that's on the shop
